introduction_structure.main_message: Introduction In recent years, conditional diffusion models (CDMs) have achieved notable success.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Builds context and advances the paper's argument.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Introduction In recent years, conditional diffusion models (CDMs) have achieved notable success. Their impact is particularly vis- ible across a range of low-level vision tasks [6]. This in- cludes advancements in image generation [36], inpainting [17], virtual dressing [23] and person synthesis[21, 22]. The achievements, from foundational works [9, 24, 27, 28] to cutting-edge models [4, 19, 35], highlight their versatility and effectiveness.

introduction_structure.main_message: Traditional conditional diffusion mod- els, while effective, face challenges due to their reliance on training.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Traditional conditional diffusion mod- els, while effective, face challenges due to their reliance on training. These models often require additional train- ing data and computational resources, which can be costly and limit their broader application. *Corresponding author. Dhariwal et al. [4] pioneered the use of pre-trained dif- fusion models for conditional generative task by introduc- ing ∇zt log pt(zt|y) = ∇zt log pt(zt) + ∇zt log pt(y|zt), In this framework, given condition y and latent zt at the time step t, the pre-trained diffusion prior ∇zt log pt(zt) serves as a foundation for the generative process and the gradient of the time-dependent classifier ∇zt log pt(y|zt) helps steer the generation towards the desired condition, then the condition-matching score ∇zt log pt(zt|y) aligns the generated content with the condition. Building on this foundation, recent works [1, 2, 32, 34, 39], often termed as training-free methods, eliminate the need for re-training or fine-tuning the diffusion prior and have skillfully uti- lized differentiable loss functions to approximate the con- ditional probability pt(y|zt).

introduction_structure.main_message: However, the approximation strategy can disrupt the stability of the sampling method and requires extensive experimentation to determine opti- mal settings.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: However, the approximation strategy can disrupt the stability of the sampling method and requires extensive experimentation to determine opti- mal settings. This interdependence between approximation and sampling highlights their complex relationship and the need for a more robust solution. The sampling algorithm of diffusion models consists of two primary elements: an unconditional latent µt+1, which satisfies ∇µt+1 pt(µt+1) = 0 to maximize the distribution pt(zt), and a Gaussian noise ϵt+1. The output at step t is represented as zt = µt+1+σt+1ϵt+1, where σt+1 be a con- stant.

introduction_structure.main_message: To achieve conditional generation, existing methods replace the unconditional distributionpt(zt) with the condi- tional distribution pt(zt|y).
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Builds context and advances the paper's argument.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: To achieve conditional generation, existing methods replace the unconditional distributionpt(zt) with the condi- tional distribution pt(zt|y). We diverge from this approach by proposing a two-stage noise sampling process. First, we uniformly sample a condition. Then, we generate output samples based on a distribution specific to that condition.

introduction_structure.main_message: In this two-step context, we argue that zt = µt+1 + σt+1ϵt+1 remains unconditional because the sampling of ϵt+1 is not biased towards any specific condition.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Transitions from problem framing to the high-level solution.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: In this two-step context, we argue that zt = µt+1 + σt+1ϵt+1 remains unconditional because the sampling of ϵt+1 is not biased towards any specific condition. With a deterministic condition established in the first stage, devoid of random- ness, the noise in the second stage becomes conditional, introducing a directional bias yet preserving its stochastic properties. In this paper, we disclose that NoiseCtrl, com- bining µt+1 with this conditional noise, will produce a re- sult result is attentive to the given condition. This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.

method_expression: In this pa- per, we propose NoiseCtrl, a sampling-algorithm-agnostic technique for controlled image generation.

Our contributions are as follows: • We introduce a method that is the first to achieve training- free conditional generation by pioneering the transforma- tion of isotropic Gaussian noise into conditional noise. • We introduce a pioneering sampling-algorithm-agnostic method, which can be effortlessly integrated with any sampling approach for conditional generative task. • Our method demonstrates the adaptability and superior performance of our method across a spectrum of condi- tional generation tasks.
We compare our method with previous approaches using both quantitative and qualitative metrics.
Conclusion We propose the novel NoiseCtrl, a Sampling-Algorithm- Agnostic conditional sampling method designed to tackle a diverse array of conditional generation tasks.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
In training-free conditional generative tasks, diffusion
models utilize differentiable loss functions to steer the gen-
erative reverse process, necessitating modifications to sam-
pling algorithms like DDPM and DDIM. However, such ad-
justments likely reduce flexibility and reliability. In this pa-
per, we propose NoiseCtrl, a sampling-algorithm-agnostic
technique for controlled image generation. Essentially, dif-
fusion models generate denoised results zt−1 by adding a
predicted mean µt with random noise ϵt. NoiseCtrl specifi-
cally adjusts the random noise while leaving the underlying
sampling algorithms unchanged. At each step t, NoiseCtrl
converts the unconditional Gaussian noise into conditional
noise ϵ′
t by substituting the isotropic Gaussian distribution
with the von Mises–Fisher distribution. This substitution
introduces a directional focus while preserving the random-
ness required for conditional image generation. Thanks to
this non-intrusive design, NoiseCtrl is straightforward to in-
tegrate and has been extensively validated through experi-
ments, demonstrating its adaptability for different diffusion
algorithms and superior performance across various con-
ditional generation tasks.
raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
In recent years, conditional diffusion models (CDMs) have
achieved notable success. Their impact is particularly vis-
ible across a range of low-level vision tasks [6]. This in-
cludes advancements in image generation [36], inpainting
[17], virtual dressing [23] and person synthesis[21, 22]. The
achievements, from foundational works [9, 24, 27, 28] to
cutting-edge models [4, 19, 35], highlight their versatility
and effectiveness. Traditional conditional diffusion mod-
els, while effective, face challenges due to their reliance
on training. These models often require additional train-
ing data and computational resources, which can be costly
and limit their broader application.
*Corresponding author.
Dhariwal et al. [4] pioneered the use of pre-trained dif-
fusion models for conditional generative task by introduc-
ing ∇zt log pt(zt|y) = ∇zt log pt(zt) + ∇zt log pt(y|zt),
In this framework, given condition y and latent zt at the
time step t, the pre-trained diffusion prior ∇zt log pt(zt)
serves as a foundation for the generative process and the
gradient of the time-dependent classifier ∇zt log pt(y|zt)
helps steer the generation towards the desired condition,
then the condition-matching score ∇zt log pt(zt|y) aligns
the generated content with the condition. Building on this
foundation, recent works [1, 2, 32, 34, 39], often termed
as training-free methods, eliminate the need for re-training
or fine-tuning the diffusion prior and have skillfully uti-
lized differentiable loss functions to approximate the con-
ditional probability pt(y|zt). However, the approximation
strategy can disrupt the stability of the sampling method
and requires extensive experimentation to determine opti-
mal settings. This interdependence between approximation
and sampling highlights their complex relationship and the
need for a more robust solution.
The sampling algorithm of diffusion models consists of
two primary elements: an unconditional latent µt+1, which
satisfies ∇µt+1 pt(µt+1) = 0 to maximize the distribution
pt(zt), and a Gaussian noise ϵt+1. The output at step t is
represented as zt = µt+1+σt+1ϵt+1, where σt+1 be a con-
stant. To achieve conditional generation, existing methods
replace the unconditional distributionpt(zt) with the condi-
tional distribution pt(zt|y). We diverge from this approach
by proposing a two-stage noise sampling process. First, we
uniformly sample a condition. Then, we generate output
samples based on a distribution specific to that condition. In
this two-step context, we argue that zt = µt+1 + σt+1ϵt+1
remains unconditional because the sampling of ϵt+1 is not
biased towards any specific condition. With a deterministic
condition established in the first stage, devoid of random-
ness, the noise in the second stage becomes conditional,
introducing a directional bias yet preserving its stochastic
properties. In this paper, we disclose that NoiseCtrl, com-
bining µt+1 with this conditional noise, will produce a re-
sult result is attentive to the given condition.
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.
raw_sections.method:
18093

Our approach distinguishes itself from prior methods by
eliminating the need to approximate pt(zt|y). Instead, we
focus solely on adapting the random noise, with modifica-
tions that are neutral to the specific sampling algorithm em-
ployed. Our contributions are as follows:
• We introduce a method that is the first to achieve training-
free conditional generation by pioneering the transforma-
tion of isotropic Gaussian noise into conditional noise.
• We introduce a pioneering sampling-algorithm-agnostic
method, which can be effortlessly integrated with any
sampling approach for conditional generative task.
• Our method demonstrates the adaptability and superior
performance of our method across a spectrum of condi-
tional generation tasks.
raw_sections.experiments:
5. Experiment
In this section, we evaluate our methods through a variety of
experiments. We start by detailing the experimental setup.
We then demonstrate its seamless integration with vari-
ous sampling methods, including DDPM [9], DDIM [25],
LCM [14], DPM [13], Euler [10], and SA [31], for di-
verse conditional generation tasks. We also show that Noi-
seCtrl is compatible with both training-based CMDs like
ControlNet [35], T2I-Adapter [15], and IP-Adapter [33],
and training-free CMDs such as UGD [1], FreeDom [34],
and DSG [32]. We compare our method with previous
approaches using both quantitative and qualitative metrics.
Lastly, we discuss the impact of the newly introduced pa-
rameter κ on the final results.
raw_sections.conclusion:
6. Conclusion
We propose the novel NoiseCtrl, a Sampling-Algorithm-
Agnostic conditional sampling method designed to tackle
a diverse array of conditional generation tasks. Our method
is distinguished by two key innovations: 1. We introduce
the use of conditional noise in place of Gaussian noise for
coherent generation without altering existing sampling al-
gorithms. 2. We present both fast and slow direction vec-
tor estimation methods to determine the condition of the
conditional noise, and we advocate for the use of the von
Mises-Fisher distribution instead of the uniform distribu-
tion for expedited sampling. NoiseCtrl’s versatility posi-
tions it as an excellent plug-and-play enhancement for con-
ditional diffusion models, offering substantial performance
improvements without necessitating code modifications or
significant computational overhead.
